tlos: TLO 1. Solve problems using logarithms and decibels
TLO 2. Relate numbers in different bases and convert between them.
TLO 3. Decompose real-world trigonometric problems
TLO 4. Define properties of electromagnetic waves.
TLO 5. Identify common frequency designations.
TLO 6. Locate the relative position of bands on the EM spectrum.
TLO 7. Relate aircraft dynamics with Doppler effects and required mission systems error corrections.
TLO 8. Describe the fundamentals of signal synthesis and signal analysis.
TLO 9. Describe digital sampling, quantization error, and aliasing.
TLO 10. Summarize the advantages of modulating an information signal onto a carrier.
TLO 11. Give examples showing amplitude, frequency, and phase tradeoffs in modulation.
TLO 12. Explain the purpose of the Fourier Transform and what data it yields about a signal.
TLO 13. Compare Federated and integrated avionics systems.
TLO 14. Differentiate between various categories of sensor systems.
TLO 15. Characterize the Link and Link budget analysis.

events.description: This 4-hour academic block is an overview of the essential math, physics, and engineering knowledge that span many mission systems.

The lab introduces the student to test methodologies for evaluating integrated avionics systems in the F-16 UTD. The student is exposed to an F-16 Operational Flight Program (OFP) with a deficiency, enabled to learn about the OFP update process, and required to evaluate the OFP to perform limited regression testing to determine if new deficiencies were inadvertently created.

The lab introduces the student to test methodologies for evaluating integrated avionics systems in the F-16 UTD. The student is exposed to an F-16 Operational Flight Program (OFP) with a deficiency, enabled to learn about the OFP update process, and required to evaluate the OFP to perform limited regression testing to determine if new deficiencies were inadvertently created.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand and use the language of data and machine learning scientists, and how this applies to data-driven mission systems in the DoD.
TLO 2. Understand aspects of velocity, volume, value, variety and veracity of big data-generating sources.
TLO 3. Understand the latest tools and technologies for storing, parsing and analyzing big data.
TLO 4. Understand KPIs of data-driven mission systems and know when to select the corresponding KPI to the desired outcome.
TLO 5. Apply protocols of T&E to verify data-driven mission systems performance.
TLO 6. Apply and implement techniques for large scale data analysis in Python.
TLO 7. Apply adaptive control techniques and model-free algorithms to control mission systems.
TLO 8. Collect and analyze data from mission systems using modern techniques and software.
TLO 9. Identify and analyze risks in DDMS, such as vulnerabilities in communication networks, errors in control algorithms, or sensor miscalibration.

events.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Decision Making for Mission Systems Lecture
events.description: This block will cover data-driven testing for mission systems, including a broad scope of topics that include data science, uncertainty quantification, test & evaluation, statistical hypothesis testing, and decision making. Topics covered in the 1-week block include exploratory data analysis, data visualization, structured vs unstructured data, streaming vs. post-mission data, meta-data tagging, and structured inputs and data pipelines. It will also cover key performance indicators (KPIs), system performance and predictive modelling, validation and verification in data-driven test, statistical comparisons, Experimental design, adversarial machine learning & resilience strategies, and software for T&E of data-driven mission systems. Additionally, the block will cover probabilistic reasoning, reinforcement learning, autonomous control with RL (model-free and model-based), ethical considerations, data and decision fusion techniques, and considerations for tactical vs. operational vs. strategic environments.

events.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Decision Making for Mission Systems Exam
events.description: This block will cover data-driven testing for mission systems, including a broad scope of topics that include data science, uncertainty quantification, test & evaluation, statistical hypothesis testing, and decision making.

events.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Decision Making for Mission Systems Debrief
events.description: This block will cover data-driven testing for mission systems, including a broad scope of topics that include data science, uncertainty quantification, test & evaluation, statistical hypothesis testing, and decision making.
This simulator will be a capstone event for data-driven testing of mission systems. It involves a hands-on exercise applying the skills learned throughout the program related to data-driven test.

prerequisites.name: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Mission Systems Lecture

tlos: TLO 1. Explain the academic principles underlying the operation and performance of Electro-Optical (EO) systems, including the fundamental concepts such as radiometry, photometry, detection theory / impacts on performance, and imaging/LASER systems.
TLO 2. Apply laboratory, ground, and flight techniques to evaluate and characterize the performance of EO systems in various operational environments.
TLO 3. Analyze the architecture and functionality of EO systems from a systems-level perspective, identifying key subsystems and their roles in overall system performance.
TLO 4. Compare different EO technologies and configurations based on performance metrics and application domains.
TLO 5. Design basic EO test scenarios and interpret test results to assess system effectiveness.
TLO 6. Explain the fundamental principles of electromagnetic wave propagation and their application to radar systems, including the roles of frequency, wavelength, and polarization.
TLO 7. Analyze the functions and performance trade-offs of various antenna types and array configurations used in airborne radar systems.
TLO 8. Evaluate radar detection capabilities and limitations using the radar range equation, signal-to-noise ratio, and probability of detection concepts.
TLO 9. Differentiate between range, angle, and Doppler velocity measurement techniques and assess their accuracy and applicability in various developmental test scenarios.
TLO 10. Interpret the effects of clutter, terrain, and target motion on air and ground radar tracking performance and recommend appropriate mitigation strategies.
TLO 11. Design and conduct a basic radar developmental test plan, including selection of objectives, instrumentation, data collection methods, and safety considerations.
TLO 12. Implement lecture learning to assess radar properties.

events.description: This 9-hour academic lecture course provides students with the theoretical background necessary to understand how ultraviolet, visible, and infrared electromagnetic energy is used in military applications. Several fundamental types of EO systems are presented, and their specific applications are discussed. Measures of performance and figures of merit for various systems are presented as well as specific ground and flight test methodologies used to evaluate performance.

This 9-hour academic lecture course provides students with the theoretical background necessary to understand how ultraviolet, visible, and infrared electromagnetic energy is used in military applications.

This 9-hour academic lecture course provides students with the theoretical background necessary to understand how ultraviolet, visible, and infrared electromagnetic energy is used in military applications.

This 9-hour academic lecture course provides students with the theoretical background necessary to understand how ultraviolet, visible, and infrared electromagnetic energy is used in military applications.

This 8-hour academic lecture course builds upon the Mission Systems Fundamentals lecture and provides the academic knowledge necessary to apply those fundamentals to development and test of military RF sensors. The student is introduced to radio frequency limitations, signal processing, RADAR technical measures, and mission-oriented designs specifically associated with military RADARs such as pulsed, pulse-Doppler, remote sensing and moving target detection.

This 8-hour academic lecture course builds upon the Mission Systems Fundamentals lecture and provides the academic knowledge necessary to apply those fundamentals to development and test of military RF sensors.

This 8-hour academic lecture course builds upon the Mission Systems Fundamentals lecture and provides the academic knowledge necessary to apply those fundamentals to development and test of military RF sensors.

This 8-hour academic lecture course builds upon the Mission Systems Fundamentals lecture and provides the academic knowledge necessary to apply those fundamentals to development and test of military RF sensors.

These blocks build on the RADAR academic knowledge with a hands-on laboratory and one hands-on simulator (6121S or 6122S). The first RADAR lab uses the TPS LabVolt laboratory RADAR to demonstrate theoretical RADAR/test concepts as well as basic RADAR displays (A-Scope, and PPI).
The RADAR simulator occurs in the F-22 Software Integration Lab (SIL) for cleared students. Students learn basic and advanced RADAR test techniques for A/A and A/G RADAR.
The RADAR simulator occurs in the F-16 UTD for uncleared students. Students learn basic and advanced RADAR test techniques for A/A and A/G RADAR.

This mission is designed to introduce the student to the differences between visible (TV), infrared (IR), and ground mapping RADAR sensors in the flight environment and to introduce several flight test techniques to evaluate the sensors both qualitatively and quantitatively.

This mission is designed to introduce the student to the differences between visible (TV), infrared (IR), and ground mapping RADAR sensors in the flight environment and to introduce several flight test techniques to evaluate the sensors both qualitatively and quantitatively. Knowledge gained during the EO ground test will be applied to operate a Targeting Pod in an inflight environment. The Sensors Flight provides data to support a written report due 5 business days after the last team sortie.
The Sensors Flight provides data to support a written report due 5 business days after the last team sortie.

tlos: TLO 1. Recall the critical tenants of space communication.

events.description: This lecture covers RF and Communications systems fundamentals to prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. This lecture series will discuss the basics of data encoding, the link equation, antenna fundamentals, modulation techniques and multiple access techniques.

The hands-on lab demonstrating RF and Communications systems fundamentals will be executed in combination with the Space Communications lecture. It will prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. The students will also gain familiarization with the software defined radio program, Hack RF equipment, and antenna connectivity.

The exercise is the integration event for Communications and RF instructions. The students will be presented a communications and RF scenario. They will work in small teams to develop the relevant sections of a detailed test plan which they will submit as a deliverable. They will then execute their approved test plan, collect relevant communications, and RF data, analyze their results, and submit a limited evaluation as a final graded assessment.

The exercise is the integration event for Communications and RF instructions. The students will be presented a communications and RF scenario. They will work in small teams to develop the relevant sections of a detailed test plan which they will submit as a deliverable.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand navigation theory and underlying principles.
TLO 2. Analyze reference frames in relation to air and space navigation systems.
TLO 3. Describe Inertial Navigation Systems, Inertial Measurement Systems, and methods to verify their performance.

events.description: This 8-hour academic lecture Phase introduces aircraft navigation systems, including basic theory of operation, positioning information characteristics and basic test methodology. Topics include the basic principles of navigating across earth's surface, geodetic reference frames, ground-based radio navigation, Coriolis acceleration, inertial navigation, space-based satellite navigation with special emphasis on Global Positioning System applications, and integrated navigation and guidance systems using Kalman filtering techniques.
This 8-hour academic lecture Phase introduces aircraft navigation systems, including basic theory of operation, positioning information characteristics and basic test methodology.

events.eventName: Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report
events.description: Students will be assigned groups to analyze example navigation performance data provided by the instructor in the form of a notional navigation flight test program with specific requirements and performance specifications to be assessed. The students will present their results, analysis, conclusions, and recommendations to an audience composed of simulated stakeholders including the systems program office, contractor engineers, and operational testers.

events.eventName: Navigation and State Estimation Operational Topic Brief
events.description: Students will be assigned groups to analyze example navigation performance data provided by the instructor in the form of a notional navigation flight test program with specific requirements and performance specifications to be assessed.
Students will be assigned groups to analyze example navigation performance data provided by the instructor.

modules.moduleName: Component Systems Exam and Special Topics
tlos: TLO 1. Synthesize mission systems information.

events.description: This test covers all the applicable component systems taught throughout SY 6000.

prerequisites.name: Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report

events.description: This test covers all the applicable component systems taught throughout SY 6000.

prerequisites.name: Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report

events.description: These are a number of presentations on cutting-edge test techniques and technical details currently in execution throughout the Air Force Test Center

tlos: TLO 1. Compare the results to predicted behavior stated in the system documentation.
TLO 2. Deduce what could have accounted for the anomalous behavior recorded during the given test.
TLO 3. Prioritize subject matter expert feedback to reinforce curriculum knowledge
TLO 4. Examine the electromagnetic spectrum and research how it is manipulated/interpreted to communicate/relay information.
TLO 5. Correlate the fundamental properties of physics exploited by individual mission systems and Infer how those properties either enable or limit information for the warfighter.
TLO 6. Categorize deficiencies to communicate mission relevance.
TLO 7. Integrate key mission systems into a modern battlespace to provide a mission-focused context for how to test those systems.
TLO 8. Coordinate with testers to perform an evaluation using the electromagnetic spectrum and formulate how it is manipulated/interpreted to communicate/relay information.
TLO 9. Combine the fundamental properties of physics exploited by individual mission systems and integrate those properties to generate information for the warfighter.
TLO 10. Justify risk assessment.
TLO 11. Determine overall system ability and specification compliance.

events.description: This 2-hour academic lecture phase is a survey in the process of developing human machine interfaces. Benefits and challenges of each display type are examined and topics including resolution, touchscreen displays, helmet alignment, cockpit mapping, and test methodologies are presented.

This 2-hour academic lecture phase is a survey in the process of developing human machine interfaces. Benefits and challenges of each display type are examined and topics including resolution, touchscreen displays, helmet alignment, cockpit mapping, and test methodologies are presented.

This is a 3-hour academic overview of the types of documentation and specifications common in software and networks mission systems testing. The MIL-STD-1760 interface and MIL-STD-1553 data bus protocol is introduced as well as other military and commercial data busses. This block introduces the various software development and test methodologies used by defense industry partners and the DoD for aircraft, space and cyber systems, and how requirements definition, development, and test approaches differ between legacy and more modern development frameworks.

These labs reinforce academic knowledge acquired in the lecture with a 1-hour lab conducted with 4 students at a time. Students can manipulate code on a stand-alone computer to illustrate vulnerabilities such as buffer overflows and race conditions. They are also exposed to automated testing using CI/CD pipelines. Additionally, students demonstrate the performance differences between programming languages (compiled and interpreted), virtualization technologies (virtual machines and containers), Internet Protocol (IP) network data transfers (TCP and UDP), and cryptographic algorithms (AES and RSA) to better internalize the tradeoffs and promise of the cutting-edge computer science currently being used in modern mission systems architectures.

This block discusses vehicles as a system of systems, then decomposes individual systems using the design process down to specific mission systems, mission essential functionality, and cyber-physical systems technologies. This provides a context necessary to understand how information flow within cyber-physical systems and physical technology shapes integrated avionics/regression testing as well as cyber vulnerability analysis.

This 2-hour lecture is an introduction to the unique testing considerations for unmanned platforms from a mission systems point of view. Subjects covered will include RPA common classifications, system components and traits, and unique test considerations.

events.description: This 8-hour academic lecture course introduces students to the fundamental aspects of air-to-ground and air-to-air weapons and weapons integration testing. Topics include weapon components, guided weapons delivery testing, guidance modes, release point and impact point calculation, error budgets, safe separation, DT/OT teaming, some specialized weapons testing and instrumentation.
The 2-hour field trip to the Edwards AFB weapons load hangar gives students hands-on experience with many of the weapons they may encounter during a test campaign.
A 1-hour MIB outlines the flight and mission control room events.

prerequisites.name: F-15 E/EX Advanced Sensors Control Room

events.description: For the simulator event, students will be issued flight test weapon cards to practice systems test conduct, as well as discuss system management, error propagation, and systems test safety. Student teams will practice test/control division of duties and responsibilities, while offering a first look at the F-16 weapons delivery event.

prerequisites.name: F-15 E/EX Advanced Sensors Control Room

events.description: The sortie will consist of three main areas: 1) Operational release of inert GBU-12 with self-lasing to a stationary target. 2) JDAM regression testing and DLZ analysis. 3) F-16 avionics regression testing. During the sortie, students will sharpen their applied weapons test conduct foundations and will be introduced to weapons telemetry in the control room.

events.description: During the sortie, students will sharpen their applied weapons test conduct foundations and will be introduced to weapons telemetry in the control room.

events.description: This sortie is designed to demonstrate close integration with the Space Test Course Systems Practical and give select students a broadening experience for working with agencies beyond their background domain.

events.eventName: F-16 Weapons Delivery Domain Integration Working Group
events.description: FTC students will work closely with STC students to plan and execute a precision Time-On-Target GBU-12 weapon delivery to correspond with commercial satellite EO/IR pass. FTC and STC students will be scheduled for a 1-hour mission planning cell at minimum.

Pilots selected should be primarily those with an F-16 background (primary) or those with a background dropping GBU-12s (secondary). FTC students will also accomplish JDAM regression and DLZ analysis, along with any other regression cards in the time and fuel remaining.

events.eventName: F-16 Weapons Domain Integration Control Room
events.description: FTEs or CSOs selected should be those with previous control room experience or weapons delivery experience.
Students will give an oral debrief of their JDAM DLZ error analysis as a group using relevant data collected during the F-16 flight (and sim if applicable).

Students will observe imagery obtained against non-traditional targets used for engineering measurements and collect qualitative usability data for a new sensor interface. Students are expected to analyze, evaluate, and debrief the data gathered during the execution event.

events.description: Students are required to select appropriate targets for sensor and weapons testing, develop a mission profile and cards to accomplish a limited developmental test and evaluation of the MQ-9 sensors and ground weapons delivery system. Students receive an MQ-9 controls and displays orientation in a ground control segment (GCS) simulator.

events.description: The mission phase is conducted using either a real MQ-9 or GCS simulator depending on aircraft availability.

events.description: Students provide an expanded oral debrief of results after the mission.

events.description: Students are provided a test plan to evaluate a "new" F-16 RADAR OFP. This OFP also requires a limited safety of flight regression. Pilots and FTEs will work as a team to research system operation, write test cards, execute the mission (FTE directing from control room), and provide an expanded oral debrief of results after the mission.

events.description: This practical will integrate the material presented in the mission systems platform course. Students will execute the exercise and complete a written report and oral presentation.

Students will develop the test approach based on a scenario provided. Instructors will provide limited coaching and students will be expected to work in small teams to complete event. Multi-domain aspects of T&E will be considered. The project will integrate and reinforce the materials presented up to this point in the curricula and give the students an opportunity to execute a test project from beginning to end. Students will be tasked to evaluate a relevant space system. Students will be required to develop a test plan for review and approval. Students will then conduct the test using the assets available for the evaluation, analyze the data and draft a final report of the results.
Students will develop the test approach based on a scenario provided. Students will execute the exercise and complete a written report and oral presentation.

TLO 3. Combine the fundamental properties of physics exploited by individual mission systems and integrate those properties to generate information for the warfighter.

events.description: This 3-hour academic lecture introduces students to the fundamental concepts of communications and datalinks systems in modern aircraft. The course reviews the types of information that is communicated using communications technologies, introduces the basic technical performance measures shared by digital communications systems, fundamental test techniques used for all communications systems, and introduces datalink test fundamentals.
This 3-hour academic lecture introduces students to the fundamental concepts of communications and datalinks systems in modern aircraft.
This 3-hour academic lecture introduces students to the fundamental concepts of communications and datalinks systems in modern aircraft.

tlos: TLO 1. Describe the functions served by a fusion system to an "integrated system".
TLO 2. Describe some advantages and disadvantages of a fusion system.
TLO 3. Define a simple fusion model and the concepts of data alignment, correlation, estimation, and identification.
TLO 4. Describe the role of sensor management in a fusion system.
TLO 5. Describe approaches to testing sensor fusion.
TLO 6. Describe some common measures of fusion engine performance.

events.description: This 2-hour academic lecture introduces data fusion models, methods, examples, and test techniques. The course builds on individual systems and sensors knowledge to teach how sensors can be integrated and fused into a user-friendly interface.
This 2-hour academic lecture introduces data fusion models, methods, examples, and test techniques.
A 2-hour laboratory is held in the F-35 simulator for US students. The Eglin F-35 simulator gives the students hands-on experience using modern integrated avionics.

tlos: TLO 1. Recall concepts of advanced weapons.

events.description: This 1-hour academic lecture will introduce students to the principles of advanced, network-enabled weapons test techniques.
This 1-hour academic lecture will introduce students to the principles of advanced, network-enabled weapons test techniques.

tlos: TLO 1. Explain the goal of Electromagnetic Warfare and the characteristics of the EW environment.
TLO 2. Describe Electromagnetic Attack, Electromagnetic Protect, Electromagnetic Support.
TLO 3. Describe the enemy "Kill Chain" (targeted against us).
TLO 4. Describe how different types of radars contribute information to an Integrated Air Defense System (IADS).
TLO 5. Abstract the impact of threat system modernization on the threat improvement/countermeasure improvement cycle.

events.description: This 7-hour academic lecture introduces the student to basics of Electromagnetic Warfare (EW) and EW test and evaluation. The course then builds on the RADAR and communications courses with respect to those electromagnetic properties that support information flow to various sensors and how EW techniques can be used to interfere with that information flow. The course introduces the concept of Integrated Air Defense Systems (IADS) and the "kill chain" used by both offensive and defensive weapons. The course continues with Electromagnetic Warfare and EW testing, followed by Low Observable (L-O) technology and L-O testing.
This 7-hour academic lecture introduces the student to basics of Electromagnetic Warfare (EW) and EW test and evaluation.

events.eventName: Electronic Warfare Simulator for Cleared Students (F-22 SIL)
events.description: This simulator builds on the academics from the fundamentals and EW lecture to provide hands-on experience testing active electronic attack equipment in a laboratory environment. US students participate in a 2-hour simulator at the F-22 SIL to demonstrate concepts, capabilities, and test techniques in a multi-ship, elevated security setting.

events.eventName: Electronic Warfare Lab for Uncleared Students (Mission Systems Lab)
events.description: This simulator builds on the academics from the fundamentals and EW lecture to provide hands-on experience testing active electronic attack equipment in a laboratory environment.
